Takeaway

Invesco MSCI EAFE Income Advantage ETF offers a high yield from an international equity portfolio and equity-linked notes embedding options strategies. EFAA looks attractive for income investors, but its track record is too short for long-term assessment. Older options income ETFs have a tendency to underperformance. Additionally, tax efficiency depends on an investor’s situation. Nonetheless, investors who like derivative income and already hold U.S.-focused ETFs may find in EFAA international diversification with contained volatility.

data.title: Coty: Challenged And Interesting At The Same Time
data.description:
My last take on Coty Inc. (COTY) dates back to 2020, when the company entered into a complicated deal with KKR & Co. (KKR), all designed to reduce and manage leverage. The company partially delivered on that, although in the sense that after initial optimism post-pandemic, shares have been greatly pressured five years later, as secular declines continue to depress the business and shares.

With Coty addressing debt and aiming to stabilize the business, all while shares are down to fresh lows, the situation looks interesting, yet massive disappointments in the past and the overhang of its majority stakeholder limit my enthusiasm here.

A Quick Trip Down Memory Lane

Coty has been a textbook example of how shareholder value has been destroyed over the past years after a rich corporate history, with the business founded back in 1904 in Paris. A decade ago, Coty was still a stock trading in its thirties when the company announced a transformative deal to acquire Procter & Gamble's beauty business in a near $12 billion deal. Forwarding five years in time, shares are trading at just four dollars.

Following that deal, the company ended up posting $7.6 billion in sales for the year ending in 2017 and fell to the low $6 billions in 2019, ahead of the pandemic arriving on the horizon. Battling declining sales, reporting highly complicated earnings, and dealing with a massive debt load, the company was driven into the hands of KKR in the spring of 2020.

At the time, Coty sold $750 million of convertible preferred shares to KKR, while it sold a 60% stake in the Professional Beauty and Retail Hair business in a $4.3 billion deal, valued at 12 times EBITDA. The terms around this sale were a bit complex, with the preferred equity issuance coming with a 9% coupon and potential dilution seen at just $6 per share, creating a real overhang on the business.

Any raised cash and reduction in debt were more than welcome, as the business operated with over an $8 billion net debt load. 760 million shares outstanding granted the business just over a $3 billion equity valuation, that was severely depressed by leverage, as trailing EBITDA just surpassed a billion, with the direction of travel clearly being down.

Following the sale, I could see net debt fall to $4.7 billion, yet preferred equity would still be introduced to the capital structure, while the business would become a lot smaller. Furthermore, the cost of financing was expensive, the operating performance saw continued struggles, and frequently charges were coming up to earnings.

Recovering & Falling

Since the pandemic, shares of Coty quickly recovered to the $10 mark in 2021 and have largely traded around those numbers until the summer of 2024. Over the past year and a half, shares have gradually fallen back to just the $3 mark, the lowest levels since the pandemic.

Forwarding to August of this year, Coty reported a 4% fall in fiscal 2025 sales to $5.89 billion, with like-for-like sales down 2%. Results were very tough to read into, with GAAP losses of $381 million coming in at $0.44 per share. Adjusted earnings were reported at $0.22 per share, this number down fifteen cents from the year before.

The company reported net debt at $3.75 billion, but after factoring in a $1.00 billion equity stake in Wella, economic net debt is reported at $2.75 billion, with interest expenses reported at $214 million for the year. With EBITDA reported at $1.08 billion, leverage ratios are seen in the mid-3s; that is if we exclude Wella, but factoring in that value, leverage has come down a lot more.

However, the company is still far from being out of the woods, as Coty guided for about a 7% like-for-like decline in first-quarter sales and a 4% decline in the second quarter. Growth was seen returning in the second half of the year, with none of this being very reassuring.

After announcing a strategic review of its consumer beauty business and raising new debt at 5.6% coupons over the fall, the company reported first-quarter results early in November. First quarter reported sales fell 6% to $1.58 billion, aided by a softer dollar, with like-for-like sales down 8%. Adjusted EBITDA was down 18% to $296 million, with adjusted earnings down a fifth to $0.12 per share. Net debt ticked up to $3.8 billion, excluding its 25.8% stake in Wella, which was still valued at around a billion, as the situation looks stable in the sense that it is expected in line with previous guidance issued.

A Disappointing Sale

In the second half of December 2025, Coty announced the sale of its remaining 25.8% equity stake in Wella to KKR. A headline $750 million price tag looks soft compared to the carried value of just over a billion in its books. However, it comes with a 45% share of the proceeds from a further sale or IPO of these activities. However, this is only after preferred returns of KKR are met.

Of interest is that the company sees strong potential for additional cash proceeds to bring gross proceeds closer to the carried value. To me this translates to the company hoping that it can fetch a couple of hundred million more from this sale in the periods to come.

Currently, the 876 million shares represent an equity valuation just shy of $3 billion, trading around the $3 mark. This excludes about $3 billion in net debt load following the sale of Wella, which indicates that the business is valued at $6 billion. Let this sink in for a moment: this comes after it paid twice this amount to acquire Procter's beauty business a decade ago!

So the real question is if real value can be derived from this current $6 billion revenue base. This now applies to a business that generates nearly $6 billion in sales, about a billion in EBITDA, as, frankly, this looks cheap. The business will become simplified with the Wella sale, as this business is really comprised of two reimagining segments.

The one is a $3.8 billion prestige segment, one posting flattish sales in 2025 with adjusted margins reported at 20%. A $2.1 billion beauty segment has been struggling with like-for-like sales down 4%, and margins reported at a dismal 4%, as this business is now being structured and alternatives are explored.

This is the job for the incoming CEO, Markus Strobel, who, ironically, is a P&G veteran recently hired to drive improvements into the business in 2026 and beyond.

And Now?

There is one elephant still to be elaborated upon, in between operating headwinds, which include license losses, and that is JAB is holding a slight majority stake in the business. This might create a tough spot for minority shareholders.

This certainly is the case as the business turns, as a buyout could limit the potential for minority common equity holders here. Given all this, also, as JAB's track record in food companies might be good, that is not necessarily the same for minority equity holders who went along for various rides.

This makes me a bit cautious about COTY, as I think that right moves are made, yet in between a deplorable past decade and the overhang of JAB being a controlled owner, I am cautious. I am not willing to commit to the shares here, even as risk-reward seems to have improved meaningfully.

data.title: Stoneridge: Expect Near-Term Pain, But Long-Term Gain
data.description:
One of the most interesting companies to me right now has got to be Stoneridge, Inc. (SRI). There's a very good chance that you've never heard of the business before. But there is a very high chance that you own one of its products. In essence, the company focuses on producing automotive electronics products. Right now, I am not feeling optimistic about the automotive space. In fact, it is my assertion that 2026 would be a rather unpleasant year for shareholders in most automotive oriented companies. However, when it comes to this particular play, there are some catalysts that justify optimism. Although this year is looking worse than last year was, the stock is still cheap on an absolute basis. On top of this, management expects significant growth over the next couple of years. And even if the company can achieve even half of this growth, the upside would be monumental. It is in this context that I believe that, even though the stock is down 27.9% since I reaffirmed it as a ’buy’ candidate, that I believe that maintaining it as such makes sense here.

Taking On Stoneridge A Test Drive

Given the time that has passed since my last article about Stoneridge, we have only seen data covering one additional operating quarter come out. And that happens to be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company amounted to $210.3 million. That's unfortunately down from the $213.8 million the company reported a year earlier. Two of its three operating segments reported weakness here.

The largest of these, the Electronics segment, focus is on producing vision systems, connectivity and compliance solutions, control modules, and a wide array of other products, for the automotive space. And the year over year, revenue for this unit dropped from $127.5 million to $121.5 million. Management attributed this decline to the lower customer production volumes throughout North America and Europe when it came to commercial vehicles. But honestly, this is not surprising to me. The commercial vehicle space has come under some meaningful pressure this year.

Tariffs have been a big cause of this, but also when it comes to the semi-truck space, there has been weakness following over building in that market in order to address supply chain issues after economies reopened following the end of the COVID-19 pandemic. These issues, combined with a gradual weakening of the economic climate in the US, is causing experts to forecast commercial vehicle market volumes to be lower by 6.4% this year in Europe and to be lower by 27.7% this year in North America. So it stands to reason that we would see revenue drop here.

Also on the decline ended up being the Control Devices segment, which produces and sells actuators, sensors, switches, connectors, and other related products. Revenue for this segment dropped from $73.1 million to $71.6 million. This involved weakness not only in the North American automotive market, but also in China, where revenue declined by $2.1 million year over year. Interestingly, even though there was general weakness here, the segment did benefit from higher sales in the commercial vehicle markets. But that did not stop profitability from declining from $2.1 million to $1.2 million.

Lastly, we have the one bright spot for the company, which was its smallest segment. This is none other than Stoneridge Brazil, which involves its Brazilian operations, as its name suggests. For new for this unit actually surged from $13.2 million to $17.1 million. And with that rise came an increase in profits from $0.7 million to $2.7 million. Higher demand for OEM products seems to have been the driver here. But even with what can only be described as rapid growth, with revenue jumping 29.7% year over year, the overall small size of this segment was not enough to offset the weakness in the other two segments.

This is already a low margin space to play in. So when you see a decline in revenue, a decline in profits and cash flows is almost unavoidable. Sure enough, net income worsened from negative $7.1 million to negative $9.4 million. Operating cash flow plunged from $10.7 million to $3.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a worsening from $4.3 million to negative $1.3 million. In fact, the only profitability metric to improve was EBITDA. And it only ticked up slightly from $9.2 million to $9.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have all worsened across the board. And the two largest operating segments remained a problem during this time. Near term, management expects this weakness to continue. Revenue for 2025 in its entirety is expected to be between $860 million and $870 million.

This is actually down from the previous range of between $860 million and $890 million, with that downward revision almost certainly playing a role in the plunge in price as the stock endured. For context, last year, revenue was $908.3 million. We can also expect profitability to worsen. Management expects, as an example, for EBITDA this year to be between $30 million and $32 million. Last year, it was $37.9 million. And in all likelihood, this would translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $15.2 million compared to the $18.6 million reported last year.

Normally, this kind of performance would turn me off, especially as the US economy seems to be heading toward a recession. After all, recessionary conditions, especially ones plagued by tariff induced inflation, should not bode well for the automotive space. But there are a couple of reasons why I am optimistic. One of the big ones is its MirrorEye product line, which is a camera monitor system that has become incredibly popular. In essence, it provides additional visibility to drivers, making the driving experience safer. For the first nine months of this year, sales of this product were up 78% compared to the same time a year earlier. And the company continues to receive awards for it.

I detailed how, in the second quarter of this year, the company received a multi-year contract worth $535 million. This should bring in peak revenue of $140 million for the best year of that specific contract. In the third quarter of this year, the company received another award from an OEM in the amount of $55 million. And that particular program is expected to launch in the first quarter of 2028. The company has achieved other wins as well. This includes, but is not limited to, a multi-year park lock actuator program award of $130 million. And it also involves a second program awarded for its leak detection module for an undisclosed sum.

From all that I see, there does not appear to be any update regarding what the company expects for the long term. But when I last wrote about the business, they said that EBITDA is likely to grow to $70 million next year before expanding to between $160 million and $200 million by 2029. This would likely mean adjusted operating cash flow of $34.4 million next year and $88.5 million in 2029.

Using these estimates, as well as the historical results of the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. The multiples today look fine, but they aren't exceptional. But if management can hit these targets for 2026 and 2029, then shares would look dirt cheap. Before I get into what kind of upside potential might exist here, I would first like to point you to the table below. In it, you can see how the company is currently priced compared to five other similar firms. This is exactly why I say that the company does not look exceptional at the moment. And it's because three of the five companies that I compared it to on a price to operating cash flow basis are cheaper than it is. And four of them are cheaper when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Stoneridge 11.4 9.4
Patrick Industries (PATK) 13.2 11.9
Strattec Security Corporation (STRT) 4.6 6.4
Motorcar Parts of America (MPAA) 3.6 6.6
Holley (HLLY) 11.6 8.9
Standard Motor Products (SMP) 10.1 6.9

In the event that management can deliver on its promises, the amount of upside potential for investors is difficult to understate. In the chart below, you can see implied annual upside if it continues to trade at its current multiples and delivers on its goals for 2026 and 2029. It also does the same thing under the assumption that it trades at the average of the other comparable firms. Even projecting out to 2029, we would be looking at upside of between 44.1% per year and 73.6%. And near term, we would be looking at even stronger potential. Even if current weakness in the market persists, and the company delivers on even half of this at the low end, that would be significantly greater than what the broader market would likely experience.

Takeaway

Normally, with numbers like what we are seeing with Stoneridge, I would be bullish enough to rate the company a ‘strong buy’. But there are a couple of reasons for my hesitancy to do so. For starters, I do think that economic conditions will worsen and will hurt the company. In fact, I would actually be surprised if 2026 estimates turn out to be accurate. This near term pain could harm upside for shareholders. On top of this, while the stock is cheap on a forward basis, it's not really as cheap today as I would prefer it to be. But with the wins that the company has been capturing, I do think that it justifies a very solid ‘buy’ rating.

data.title: Rocket Pharmaceuticals: Deep Dislocation Offers Asymmetric Upside On LAD-I Approval And Amended Danon Program
data.description:
Rocket Pharmaceuticals (RCKT)

Rating: Buy

Price Target (DCF): $17

Current Price: $3.54

Executive Summary: Deep Dislocation Offers Asymmetric Upside

We are initiating coverage on Rocket Pharmaceuticals with a Buy rating and a $17 price target (2-3 years). The company has an enterprise value, EV, of $151M at present, a valuation that implied near failure of its pipeline after the FDA hold on its Danon's disease program earlier this year due to an adverse event. FDA lifted the clinical hold on Danon's disease program, later allowing four more patients to be enrolled in the pivotal trial (enrollment will start in H1 2026). Investors appear to be ignoring that the FDA allowed Danon's disease program to resume enrollment and the high probability of near-term approval for its Leukocyte-Adhesion Deficiency-1 (LAD-1) therapy, Kresaldi.

Our bullish thesis is based on two pillars:

Upcoming LAD-1 Approval (PDUFA on March 28, 2026): A highly de-risked asset with a high probability for approval could generate ~$150M in non-dilutive capital through a Priority Review Voucher (PRV) sale (based on historical comparables: 1, 2, 3).

Danon's Disease enrollment resumption: FDA allowed resumption of the Danon's disease pivotal program after an amended protocol, which is likely to prevent a recurrence of adverse events.

Our DCF valuation provides a $700M risk-adjusted NPV for the above two programs, providing enough upside from current levels.

Danon's Disease (RP-A501): The High-Value Swing Factor

The Opportunity: Danon's disease is an X-linked cardiomyopathy with no disease-modifying treatments. Usually, these children need heart transplants by adolescence. RP-A501 is a gene therapy that uses an AAV9 vector and delivers a functional copy of the LAMP2B gene to heart cells. It has shown efficacy in the Phase 1 trial in improving cardiac remodeling and survival.

The Safety Overhang and Resolution: The stock fell over 70% in May 2025 after the death of a patient due to a systemic infection after being under treatment for capillary leak syndrome in the pivotal trial. This event was considered due to a new immunosuppressant (C3 inhibitor) that was introduced in the pivotal Phase 2 trial. The company amended the protocol, removing the new immunosuppressant. The safer Phase 1 immunosuppressant regimen with a lower dose of the gene therapy was introduced in the amended protocol, which led to the FDA removing the clinical hold in August 2025.

Status: Pivotal Phase 2 trial to start in H1 2026

Risk-assessment (Monte Carlo simulation): Our modeling for a recurrent fatal adverse event in the amended protocol pivotal trial shows an 80-90% probability of no recurrent fatal event. The risk of a recurrent fatal adverse event is only 10-20%; however, the market is pricing in this low probability in the stock price at present.

LAD-1 (Kresaldi/RP-L201): The Near-Term Validator

The Asset: LAD-1 is an ultra-rare pediatric immunodeficiency disorder that causes recurrent bacterial infections and a 60-75% mortality rate by the age of two years. Kresaldi is a gene therapy that acts by delivering a functional copy of the ITGB2 gene.

Clinical Profile: Data from the Phase 1/2 trial showed 100% survival at ≥12 months and a durable cure of immunodeficiency.

Regulatory Path: FDA has accepted the revised BLA for the asset and assigned a PDUFA date of March 28, 2026. We assign an 80% probability of success for the approval.

Strategic Value: While the total addressable market is small (~$100M peak revenue), FDA approval would unlock a Rare Pediatric Disease Priority Review Voucher (PRV), which could be worth $150M on sale.

Status: PDUFA date on March 28, 2026.

Valuation: A 'Free' Pipeline

Our risk-adjusted DCF model highlights a massive disconnect between the current valuation and intrinsic value.

Danon's disease DCF: 15K-30K patients in the US+EU, ~10-20% (2K-4K) are candidates for gene therapy. Launch: US (2029), EU/RoW (2030). Pricing: $2.5M net, similar to gene therapies like Zolgensma, Elevidys, etc. Probability of success: 60%.

Using these inputs, we forecast $700-$750M peak revenue around 2033-34. Using a discount rate of 12%, our estimate for the risk-adjusted NPV for Danon's disease is $440M.

Danon DCF table

Year Treated pts (Danon) Revenue ($M) Risk-adj FCF ($M) Discount factor Discounted FCF ($M)
2030 150 375.0 75.0 0.567 42.6
2031 225 562.5 112.5 0.507 57.0
2032 275 687.5 137.5 0.452 62.2
2033 300 750.0 150.0 0.404 60.6
2034 300 750.0 150.0 0.361 54.1

LAD-I (Kresladi) DCF:

Prevalence: 1-9/million or ~800-1000 patients in the US+EU, ~30-40% are severe. US launch: 2027, EU/RoW launch: 2028. Annual price: $3M net. Probability of success: 80%. PRV value: $150M.

Our forecast for peak risk-adjusted revenue in this indication is $105M. Including the PRV, we estimate ~$250M in risk-adjusted NPV from this indication.

LAD-I DCF table

Year Treated pts (LAD-I) Revenue ($M) Risk-adj FCF ($M) Discount factor Discounted FCF ($M)
2027 35 105.0 42.0 0.797 33.5
2028 35 105.0 42.0 0.712 29.9
2029 30 90.0 36.0 0.636 22.9
2030 25 75.0 30.0 0.568 17.0
2031 20 60.0 24.0 0.508 12.2

Combining the risk-adjusted NPV from the above two indications, we arrive at ~$690-$700M, which leaves enough room for upside compared to the current EV of only $151M.

Bear case scenario: In a bear case scenario, we modeled the recurrence of a fatal adverse event in the pivotal Danon's disease trial, thus reducing the probability of success of that program to 20%. Even in that bear case, the risk-adjusted NPV from the above two programs is ~$400M, still providing enough upside.

The company had ~$223M in cash reserves at the end of Q3 2025 with no long-term debt. Operating cash use was $202M in the last 12 months. I don't anticipate any need for a capital raise at least until the upcoming PDUFA in March.

This valuation does not include any value for:

  • AAV cardiomyopathy programs RP-A601 (PKP2-ACM) and RP-A701 (BAG3-DCM), or

  • Hematology assets (Fanconi anemia, PKD) beyond LAD-I.

Risks to the thesis

If another fatal adverse event occurs in the amended pivotal Danon's disease trial, this program would face a severe setback. The LAD-1 regulatory pathway had a prior CRL due to manufacturing issues. A second CRL would push the launch past the PRV sunset date in September 2026, destroying $150M in value. The cash runway should last until Q2 2027. If PRV is not monetized due to Kresaldi approval delay, dilutive financing will likely be required.

Final Recommendation

Rocket Pharmaceuticals is a Buy. The market has priced in the fear of a recurrent safety event in the pivotal Danon's disease trial but has failed to price in the above fix. With a near-term regulatory event (LAD-1) expected to shore up the balance sheet and a high-reward asset starting a pivotal trial in H1 2026 with a protocol amendment, the risk-reward is heavily skewed to the upside.

data.title: Why I Just Bought Some Nvidia Stock
data.description:
Despite maintaining my relatively bullish stance, I was out of Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) stock for about six months. In fact, I only initiated my most recent Nvidia position about a week ago, as the price dropped into my $170-175 buy-in zone.

Nvidia 1-Year Chart

Nvidia's stock has essentially been dead money over the last six months or so, which is partly why I've been on the sidelines recently. There simply were better stocks to be in after the late summer run-up. Nvidia also put in a clear near-term top (potentially) around the crucial $200-210 resistance level.

This dynamic actually set Nvidia up as a solid near-term short, as we had a negative RSI divergence with worsening near term sentiment. Subsequently, Nvidia went through a textbook 20% correction, which appears reasonable for the stock's longer-term trajectory.

In fact, Nvidia may have put in a double bottom around $170, and it's possible that the stock may begin another move higher from here. After all, Nvidia essentially just went through an extended consolidation phase, which lasted approximately six months. Now, as there is increasing probability Nvidia may go higher, I want to be in this stock.

Why Nvidia Could Continue Higher

For better or for worse, Nvidia remains the King of AI. I discussed the company's recent earnings in this article, and the results as well as the guidance appear like another home run, in my view.

At the end of the day, investors want results, and Nvidia provides them. We witnessed a solid double beat in the recent earnings along with excellent guidance.

Nvidia's sales continue surging due to the continued enormous demand for high-quality AI chips. Just for context, it reported $57B in sales relative to the expected approximate $55B. So, almost a $2B beat there. Also, Nvidia guided to $65B in sales for Q4 vs. the expected $61.84B figure. Additionally, the company guided to an extremely solid non-GAAP 75% gross margin for this quarter.

Still, despite these stellar earnings results, the market didn't push Nvidia's stock higher post earnings. Instead, we witnessed a sharp reversal. In my view, this is likely because the AI market needs "deflationary" periods where technical conditions and valuations in stocks like Nvidia and others must cool off.

What About After The Cool Off Period?

Well, here we are, Nvidia and many other top AI stocks have cooled off. Now what? For one, it's crucial that we've seen a solid 20% correction in Nvidia's stock, as this dynamic has essentially reset Nvidia's overextended technical situation. Also, Nvidia is not alone here. Here are some other statistics of key AI stocks correcting recently.

Broadcom (AVGO) provided solid results, and its stock dropped by about 22% from its recent high point (peak to trough). Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) declined by approximately 25%. Marvell (MRVL) went through a similar 25% decline recently.

Some AI-related companies have gotten punished much worse. Just look at Oracle (ORCL). This is typically a relatively stable company. Yet, its stock recently collapsed by 50% from its near-term overpriced level.

More speculative AI stocks like Nebius (NBIS), CoreWeave (CRWV), IREN (IREN), and others have collapsed by 50-70% or more in some cases. Now, it's not likely that many of these key AI players are facing near-term liquidity issues, or something extremely negative like fundamental structural issues.

On the contrary, many of these more speculative AI stocks and many of the current market leading companies could continue to do well, despite the recent deflationary phase in the AI space.

After all, key AI services players like Nebius, IREN, and others' revenues could skyrocket in future years. This dynamic may lead to considerable profitability down the line, and the market may continue rewarding high-quality AI leading stocks as we head into the new year.

Nvidia Doesn't Appear Expensive Anymore

Now that the technicals in the AI space, in general, look much better here, let's look at the fundamentals in Nvidia now. Here's the thing. Despite all of the circular financing, the growth will likely continue.

Nvidia has been very consistent at delivering and surpassing EPS and sales estimates, a dynamic which may continue moving forward. Also, I want to highlight that Nvidia trades at below 25 times "next year's" EPS estimates. However, now that it's about to be 2026 (fiscal 2027) for Nvidia, we should really focus on 2027's (fiscal 2028) sales and EPS estimates. Nvidia's EPS are expected to be about $10 in 2027 (fiscal 2028). I want to highlight that this is cheap given the current share price, and suggests Nvidia may only be trading at about 18 forward earnings now. Also, while I understand that there is considerable uncertainty regarding Nvidia's sales and earnings ability in 2027, it's possible that Nvidia could outperform the roughly $10 consensus estimate. Therefore, in a bullish case outcome, Nvidia may be trading below 18 times forward earnings here.

Also, let's consider Nvidia's EPS growth rate potential. If we look out at the next three years, the average annual EPS growth rate could be approximately 30%. Since Nvidia's forward P/E ratio appears to only be about 18, this places Nvidia's forward price to earnings growth ratio ("PEG" ratio) at only around 0.6 here.

This PEG ratio is extremely cheap, especially for a high-quality growth company. Many other top AI chip stocks like AMD, Marvell, and others also have very low PEG ratios, suggesting that there could be substantial upside ahead in their stocks.

Of course, the enormous caveat is that the AI growth must continue and that Nvidia and other top AI chip companies must maintain relatively high profitability rates. Nonetheless, Wall Street remains bullish here.

Wall Street Remains Bullish

An interesting dynamic is that despite the 20% correction in Nvidia's stock price, price target estimates continued to increased recently. The average 12-month price target has increase to roughly $253, representing approximately 35% of potential upside from here.

Additionally, higher end estimates range up to $350, implying that Nvidia could have roughly 100% upside in the next year in a more bullish case scenario. We see a similar dynamic with AMD, MRVL, NBIS, and many other high-quality AI stocks.

Even Oracle's average price target is around $290, despite some recent analyst decreases. This dynamic implies about 50% upside potential over the next 12 months. Therefore, it appears that the near term sentiment may have gotten too negative in the AI space.

The Bottom-Line Sentiment Is Likely To Improve

The bottom line is that nothing moves only in one direction. Trees can't grow to the sky, and stocks need corrections. However, just as the sentiment may have been too bullish around the recent peaks in October and November, the AI-related sentiment may have gotten too negative recently.

There doesn't appear to be an imminent slowdown on the horizon, in my view. Otherwise, we would have noticed issues in various earnings. Instead, we see a lot of bullish momentum and substantial growth, as the AI market broadens out and begins benefiting the market across various industries. We may still be around the sixth or the seventh inning in the AI bull market, and we may still see a significant leg higher ahead as AI adaptation and applications continue increasing moving forward. Due to the bullish outlook, I am increasing my Nvidia 12-month price target to $250, and keeping my Buy rating for Nvidia's stock.

Risks To Nvidia And Others

Of course, there are risks to Nvidia and the AI market, in general. There is the risk of growth slowing, and there is the risk of too many circular deals. There are valid concerns of a potential bubble forming, which tends to have transitory deflationary phases.

The question is when does the AI market run into structural issues and when will the slowdown in growth be significant enough to damage sentiment on a longer-term basis, potentially leading to a prolonged bear market in AI related stocks.

Certainly, we must monitor the AI segment very carefully, and remain mindful of the potential longer-term top that will likely materialize at some point. However, given the easier monetary policy, and potential higher growth environment, the more significant slowdown phase may not materialize until 2027 or 2028. Still, investors should consider these and other risks carefully before investing in AI related stocks.

data.title: IREN: AI Cloud Moment Is Real, But So Are The Risks
data.description:
IREN Limited (IREN): GPU Cloud Gaining Traction Amidst AI Gold Rush

Neoclouds are all the rage now, as demand continues to exceed supply with the AI infrastructure pushing for more gains as we move into the next stage of scaling in 2026. If the forecasts suggest that multiple trillions of dollars are expected to be spent to make this blueprint a reality, the participation of the neoclouds is almost a “foregone conclusion.”

We have already seen big tech backstopping, diversion of workloads to neoclouds, and the massive surge in data center spending as a prelude to what we can expect in the next few years. The fact is, this premise apparently lies on a “simple dynamic.” With power being seen as a primary gating factor, “cryptominers already control capacity through their PPAs and existing electrical infrastructure.” Nevertheless, I believe these aren't sufficient to meet future demand if we really expect AI workloads to proliferate in the enterprise space through the rest of the decade.

Well, I think the silver lining in this bifurcation (between the accelerated AI infrastructure buildout and the somewhat “slower” enterprise adoption) is that it should afford more time for unanticipated hiccups in the physical infra development to have more time to smooth out the edges. After all, astute investors paying close heed to the supply chain snag should understand that such challenges have already fazed builders like CoreWeave (CRWV) and Oracle (ORCL), as they also undertake substantive undertakings to actualize their AI cloud ambitions.

IREN's Crypto Mining Beginnings Confer Key Advantages

Hence, I think the roadmap seems relatively robust when considering IREN Limited's opportunity in this evolving landscape. After all, having its own data center facilities amidst the supply crunch already corroborates execution expertise, and that's clearly being validated by the Microsoft (MSFT) partnership that IREN secured in early November. Having a major hyperscaler collaborate with the emerging AI cloud in its bid to assume a leadership position not only improves the market's confidence, but it also helps underpin multi-year visibility into IREN's outlook. In other words, having a marquee customer like Microsoft provides the financial stability and contractual commitments to help pave the runway for IREN to go ahead to execute its buildout.

IREN's vertically integrated strategy arguably helps lower the risks in the initial buildout phase, as highlighted by its “no third-party colocation costs or counterparty risk.” By “building and operating” its data centers, IREN's business model lowers the implied risks emanating from the inherent complexities we know are unavoidable in the supply chain. Hence, the value proposition to preeminent customers like MSFT (in my opinion) is that they can rely on IREN to execute confidently to provide them the capacity additions that they need (in a timely fashion) without being unduly burdened with the challenges of managing and weaving through the convoluted web of supply chain interdependencies.

However, vertical integration also involves complexities that entail being proficient across the whole stack. I believe bulls will contend that IREN isn'​t just executing through PowerPoint presentations per se but already has a working operating model to justify the optimism. While the optimal ROI debate between GPU as a service and colocation workarounds may continue to persist, at this point, I believe the overwhelming demand surrounding the AI narrative right now could still skew the optimism in favor of IREN for the foreseeable future.

IREN: Competition Aplenty, Sustaining Moat Remains A Key Challenge

The pivotal question is how long will this line of argument still bolster the bullish side of the ledger, knowing that there's really no shortage of competition or providers to build GPU clouds (see above table)? IREN's strategic scaling roadmap sees the company operating more than 140K GPUs by the end of 2026, a phenomenal increase from just 23K. With that, the nascent AI neocloud and concurrent crypto miner sees the opportunity to broaden its annualized recurring revenue clarity to $3.4B at full deployment.

Fortuitously, it appears that IREN has managed to secure sufficient power as part of the GPU scaling while also highlighting that it has obtained “substantial optionality” for future requirements. Also, asset reuse with “GPU swap out” has also gone rather smoothly in IREN's asset base, elevating the market's confidence in IREN's targeted capacity as compared to builders without this inherent advantage.

These are aspects of its business model that will be critical for investors to carefully ascertain as 2026 comes into view, with the AI trade currently facing its moment of reckoning as investors reassess the sustainability of the AI gold rush amid the ongoing market rotation themes.

IREN: Negative Free Cash Flow Must Be Addressed

Wall Street has already baked in a relatively successful scaling outlook through 2027, projecting >$2.7B in revenue by then. Yet, the near-term economics is clearly focused on growth for now, as negative free cash flow could persist through then. While IREN's financing and liquidity have been boosted, I believe investors should be prepared for further growth-related financing models moving forward, although I don't think it's going to scupper IREN's bullish narrative. The underlying proposition in this buildout phase is to execute the buildout accordingly to prove the rapid scaling as the foundational pillar of its business model.

However, the key question that comes to mind is whether that's enough to maintain the confidence in its exuberant valuation while the buildout continues to devour substantial cash flow through the rest of the decade. The valuation isn't just expensive, but also arguably frothy, with a forward revenue multiple of 9x (on an enterprise value basis), well above the tech median of 3.4x.

An unexpected slowdown in the AI fervor could hit these GPU clouds massively because the forward multiples implied in IREN's valuation suggest there's no room for disappointment if execution disappoints or a demand slowdown were to emerge.

Buy Or Sell IREN Stock?

That said, IREN's price action shows that the current momentum continues to favor the bulls, with dip buying clearly ascertained last week (December lows). It also marked an opportune moment for buyers to show conviction after a steep fall from the November highs, which also coincided with a bear market decline that also saw the stock collapse nearly 60% through the recent lows.

With IREN's momentum having clearly stumbled into the oversold levels, I believe buyers looking to catch the falling knife could return to support a possible recovery.

In such instances, while I would usually favor a timely entry point to buy into companies focused on the AI buildout, I'm less convinced with the sustainability of the moat underpinning emerging GPU clouds like IREN. And since the recent market rotation has also unveiled opportunities across the tech space, I believe there is no shortage of prospective buys that we can contemplate as we look forward to 2026.

Rating: Hold.

data.title: Volatility Selling Is Driving The Market Higher And May Be About To End
data.description:
The grinding market price action over the past trading week is mainly attributable to seasonal year-end implied volatility selling during the holiday-shortened trading week. The one thing the rally is not driven by is conviction.

Implied volatility for the S&P 500 has fallen to its lowest levels in a year, with the VIX now trading below 14 and shorter-dated measures trading even lower.

Volatility Suppression Driving Market Higher

More surprising is that short-dated implied volatility is falling, whereas realized volatility is rising. It most likely means that volatility could snap back sharply and suddenly. Recognizing what is taking place in the market is intended for investors of all types, particularly those concerned with timing and short-term trading. This concept was previously laid out in a Reading The Markets update on December 19.

One shorter-dated measure of implied volatility, which is the 9-day VIX, fell to 9.2 on Wednesday, December 24. It is the lowest reading since May 2024; one would have to go back to January 2020 to find a lower reading.

At the same time, the 9-day realized volatility for the S&P 500 has been steadily rising to about 11.5. Realized volatility certainly isn't high at current levels. Still, the divergence between rising realized volatility and declining implied volatility is uncommon and likely indicates that implied volatility-selling strategies are suppressing implied volatility.

The holiday-shortened trading week makes selling volatility an ideal time for the strategy, since fewer trading days mean lower risk and lead to option premium decay as time value erodes. At a time when options are settled daily on the S&P 500, there is ample opportunity for volatility-selling strategies to occur. The VIX 1-day closed at 7.4 on Wednesday, implying that implied volatility is pricing in only a 45 bps move on December 26. Going back to the beginning of index tracking, the VIX 1-day has rarely, if ever, closed below 7, so it is more likely than not that the VIX 1-day has little to no room to close at lower levels.

Hedges Are Not Unwinding

What also stands out is that, despite the decline in implied volatility, the market is not unwinding its hedges; in fact, hedging activity may be increasing, at least as indicated by the SKEW index, which measures tail risk. SKEW has risen to its highest levels since early 2025 and is back toward the upper end of its historical trading range.

Additionally, the VIX volatility index (VVIX) has fallen sharply but has begun to rise over the past two trading days as implied volatility on the VIX index has increased. Should it continue to rise, it is one of the better indicators for monitoring a reversal in implied volatility levels.

Low Trading Volume

While implied volatility naturally fluctuates within defined bounds, it is currently closer to its floor. As a result, the rally observed over the past couple of days is more a function of implied volatility selling and does not necessarily reflect bullish or bearish sentiment. In fact, volume in the S&P 500 is anaemic, and the price action is little more than a low-volume drift driven by volatility suppression.

If volatility returns and the drift ends as volumes rise and implied volatility increases, it shouldn't come as a surprise.

data.title: Red Cat's Opportunity: Capitalizing On The FCC's NDAA Enforcement
data.description:
Thesis

Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT) stock has been on a downward trend since October. The stock hit a 2025 high of about $15 before dropping almost 40% to the $9 range we see shares trading at today. Management has also outlined a pretty bullish FY25 revenue target, with a mid-point at $36 million. I see the company as needing about $20 million in Q4 to hit that target. And whilst that revenue should mostly be brought in from their military drone contracts, like the Army Short-Range Reconnaissance program, there are new market areas I see Red Cat targeting in 2026.

The Federal Communications Commission has passed a ruling which should shake up the commercial side of the drone industry, and I see Red Cat taking advantage going forward, as I'll explain. Since earnings, the stock is also up about 27%, as you can see from my previous coverage buy call. Post earnings, I expect more upside going into 2026, and I think we're starting to see that momentum now.

New FCC Ruling

As you probably know, just before Christmas, the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) announced the immediate enforcement of Section 1709 of the Fiscal Year 2025 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA). They essentially updated their Covered List to include quite a few foreign-manufactured uncrewed aircraft systems and other critical components such as communications systems, flight controllers, and sensors.

Now, as a drone manufacturer, being on the Covered List wouldn't be a good thing. When a drone or even just one of its critical components is added to the Covered List, they then can’t receive new FCC equipment authorizations, which are legally required to market/sell their products, or even to import them. So the result is that those new foreign-made drones on the Covered List are effectively banned from entering the U.S. market unless a very narrow national security exemption is granted, which would be something like a specific DoD designation.

So why the sudden switch-up? Well, this move follows a national security determination by an Executive Branch interagency body convened by the White House that these foreign-produced drones and parts pose what they call "unacceptable risks" to U.S. national security/safety. I think we're seeing a much broader effort by the White House to beef up homemade American airspace security and curb reliance on foreign technology as much as possible. It's a tailwind which started to gain momentum in 2025, especially in the drone space, back when Secretary Pete Hegseth announced his new push for U.S. drone dominance.

Competition Landscape

With such a significant ruling as this, it is important to get to know what the new competition landscape looks like now for Red Cat. The company’s products are, of course, American-made and NDAA-compliant, so they won't be negatively affected by Section 1709. Now, because the FCC ruling also applies to commercial drones, there are a couple of very big Chinese drone players who will take a hit from this news. And that's because most drones rely on wireless technologies like radio transmitters, telemetry links, Wi-Fi, cellular, and GPS-related communications. So they fall under FCC jurisdiction regardless of whether they’re used by the military or not.

DJI or Dà-Jiāng Innovations is actually the world’s largest commercial drone manufacturer. The company has a massive market share, which is often tipped to be around 75% to 80% of the U.S. consumer/commercial drone market. Now, whilst you might not have heard of that company, their products in the U.S. would be much more well-known. They sell brands like DJI Mavic, such as the Mini 5 Pro and Mavic 4 Pro. They also have the Matrice series, Phantom, and Inspire drones. These would be enterprise platforms that are used a lot for anything from filmmaking, mapping, inspection, public safety, and agriculture. And now, under the new FCC Covered List, future DJI models cannot receive FCC equipment authorizations, which means their new drones and those critical components cannot be imported or sold in the U.S. unless they, of course, obtain a national security exemption from DoD or DHS.

The other commercial player I see taking a hit would be Autel Robotics. They’re another significant Chinese competitor to DJI. Their drones would include the EVO series and other enterprise models, and are similarly impacted. It is worth noting here that existing products already authorized remain usable, but no new DJI hardware can enter the U.S. market after the listing. However, all new models from these foreign makers, even just updated versions of existing drones, cannot actually obtain new FCC approvals. So this should cut off future supply and innovation in the U.S. market.

Now, Red Cat primarily make their revenue via military contracts. Drone sales to the army, such as the Black Widow, would be what most investors are watching to see how well the company is doing. And whilst that’s true, Red Cat also has a significant commercial side to their drone business, which they’re trying to grow. After all, they make backpack drones, and the demand for them in the U.S. consumer market is quite strong. If you think back to when Red Cat acquired Teal, they essentially brought in a number of drones that serve the commercial sector, like enterprise, public safety, and industrial use. Their Teal segment builds systems like the Edge 130 VTOL, which is suitable for any aerial reconnaissance, inspection, and other commercial drone applications, in addition to just defense contracts. Now, this doesn't mean the public can get their hands on one; it means that they're available for use outside of military applications. So I see the new ruling as removing competition in that area and possibly creating new market opportunities away from purely military use, since they already hold dual-use NDAA-compliant systems.

Market Expansion

So, although Red Cat has historically focused on defense and government customers, the FCC’s enforcement of Section 1709, for me, creates a clearer path into the commercial market for the company. In 2026, I think we should see Red Cat leverage its existing NDAA‑compliant platforms like the FlightWave Edge 130 to pursue non-military applications like industrial inspections, infrastructure monitoring, public safety, and utility surveying use cases. Now, these applications have traditionally been dominated by foreign commercial drones, so going forward, it opens up a market entry point quite a lot.

The Edge 130’s VTOL is endurance-based and, of course, has modular payloads, which I see as making it well-suited for tasks like energy grid inspection or pipeline surveillance. It should also be perfect for and large‑site mapping. I also don’t expect the current Edge 103 to cross over to those applications directly, but think the company will manufacture a scaled-down system for other uses. We could see a variation of an existing drone manufactured on a per contract basis for those commercial applications. Now, we should also expect the company to make a big effort to highlight that they’re a trusted FCC‑approved drone solution. In doing this, with the right marketing, Red Cat has a great opportunity to expand its addressable market into commercial sectors that are now expected to face far more limited options for new hardware.

Valuation

Now, Red Cat has a market cap of about $1.09 billion and an enterprise value of $901 million. This is also backed up by a pretty strong cash position in the range of $206 million, with just a modest total debt figure of $22 million. On a trialing twelve month basis, the company’s price to sales ratio sits at 110x, and its EV to sales ratio sits at 121x. There’s clearly a massive premium being placed on the company for its high-growth, defense - and NDAA - compliant drone business, despite the fact that they currently only generate pretty modest revenue and have not yet produced meaningful earnings.

So, I see the valuation showing big investor expectations for some hefty top-line expansion. I also expect 2026 to bring more revenue from military contracts, as the 2025 tailwinds have shown that 2026 should be a good year for small-cap defense stocks.

In terms of stock price, Red Cat shares are now trading in the $9 range and have been on the downward slope since late October. Since that October sell, of shares are down about 10% on the 3-month chart. However, I think we're now starting to see a bit of stock recovery, with shares passing the $9 mark.

The Risk Ahead Is Hitting Guidance

So, as of the most recent earnings, it’s clear that Red Cat is still in a growth‑oriented, pre‑profit phase. Their valuation is driven more by future revenue potential and a big expected ramp-up in defense contract backlog than by current earnings. Back in 3Q25, Red Cat reported about $9.6 million in quarterly revenue, which was a hefty 646% year‑over‑year increase and a 200% jump from the prior quarter, so Red Cat is on the right track. Now, the revenue jump was mainly driven by deliveries under its U.S. Army Short‑Range Reconnaissance contract, and I still think this is going to be the main driver of growth. The commercial applications for a few of their drones won't be bringing in more than military contracts. But what it does is expand their product sales base going into 2026. We should see their existing drones be somewhat remodelled for commercial use, just to take advantage of that market.

Now, the company’s annual revenue guidance for 2025 has been updated to range between $34.5 million and $37.5 million. So the company has a lot of strong top‑line growth expectations. And by contrast, earlier quarterly results showed much smaller revenue bases and some disappointing stock sell-offs earlier in the year. For example, Q1 '25 and Q2 '25 reported just $1.7 million and $3.2 million, respectively, which goes to show the acceleration in back‑loaded contract fulfilment that has taken place throughout the year. Now, it's best to take a good look at their guidance and what Red Cat investors would be expecting come next quarter.

So, based on the company's projected annual revenue of $36 million mid-point, and the first three quarters totaling approximately $14.5 million, to now reach the low end of the full-year guidance, Red Cat would have needed about $20 million in Q4. On the other hand, reaching the high end would require about $23 million. That would actually represent the largest quarterly revenue in the company’s history. This kind of quarterly performance will depend on the fulfilment of defense and government contracts. Those contracts are milestone-based, and once drone orders have been shipped, Red Cat sees the revenue. So that would include shipments under the Army Short-Range Reconnaissance program, which management clearly sees as bringing in a big chunk of that guided revenue.

data.title: Dogecoin Holds Near $0.126 With Market Shifting From Decline To Balance
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) is trading near $0.126 on December 26, holding just above recent lows after a prolonged drawdown that has steadily unwound the speculative excess built earlier in the year. The decline, which began after the late-summer peak, has stripped DOGE of much of its momentum-driven premium.

The market’s behavior reflects balance rather than conviction, with both sides cautious after an extended unwind. What stands out now is not renewed weakness, but a noticeable loss of downside urgency. Selling pressure has eased, yet buyers remain measured, stepping in selectively rather than chasing rebounds. The result is a slow, grinding consolidation rather than a sharp relief rally.

Downtrend intact, but selling pressure fades

On the daily chart, Dogecoin remains firmly below its declining EMA structure, which continues to define the dominant trend. The 20-day EMA near $0.133 has acted as persistent overhead resistance throughout December, rejecting multiple recovery attempts. Above that, the 50-day EMA around $0.148 and the 100-day EMA near $0.169 reinforce the bearish slope, while the 200-day EMA near $0.187 highlights how far price has retraced from earlier cycle highs.

This stacked configuration confirms that the broader trend remains corrective, and recent rebounds lack the strength to challenge higher-time frame resistance. However, the character of price action has shifted. DOGE has begun to stabilize above the $0.123-0.125 zone, an area that has absorbed repeated downside tests without triggering follow-through selling. That behavior suggests seller exhaustion rather than renewed distribution, even as sentiment remains subdued.

Momentum indicators support this view. Daily RSI is holding in the high-30s to low-40s, a zone associated with stabilization rather than trend continuation. This is not a momentum regime that typically produces sharp selloffs. Instead, it often precedes range formation after prolonged declines. While there is no bullish divergence yet, the absence of further RSI deterioration points to fatigue among sellers rather than fresh bearish conviction.

Short-term structure improves but remains reactive

Lower-time frame price action adds important nuance. On the 30-minute chart, Dogecoin has flipped its Supertrend back to the upside following a liquidity-driven dip toward $0.122 earlier in the week. Parabolic SAR dots have shifted beneath price, confirming near-term stabilization and consistent dip-buying. Intraday pullbacks have been shallow, suggesting short-term demand is active at lower levels.

That said, the rebound remains contained within a broader downtrend channel. Price has yet to establish a sustained sequence of higher highs, and upside attempts continue to stall quickly. Intraday strength is reactive rather than directional, reflecting tactical positioning rather than a structural shift in trend control.

Flows and leverage signal caution, not accumulation

Spot flow data reinforces the cautious tone. Dogecoin has experienced persistent net outflows across recent months, including continued negative flows through December. The most recent readings show only modest movement, indicating that large holders are not aggressively accumulating at current levels. This explains why price is stabilizing rather than accelerating. Supply is being absorbed gradually, not chased.

Derivatives positioning remains fragile. Trading volume has increased around recent volatility, while open interest has edged lower. This combination suggests leverage is being reduced rather than rebuilt, a typical late-stage correction dynamic. Long-short ratios remain skewed toward longs across major exchanges, leaving bullish positioning exposed if price fails to reclaim resistance. Liquidation data shows long liquidations continue to outweigh shorts, confirming that optimism is still being tested rather than rewarded.

In earlier analysis, Dogecoin’s rally earlier in the year was framed as sentiment- and liquidity-driven rather than structurally supported. The subsequent drawdown has played out as expected, with leverage unwinding gradually rather than collapsing abruptly. The current consolidation aligns with that thesis, reflecting digestion of prior excess rather than the start of a new impulsive move.

Dogecoin is transitioning from decline to consolidation. The $0.123-0.125 zone remains critical support, while $0.133 and $0.148 define the first resistance levels that bulls must reclaim to shift the short-term narrative. A sustained move above the 20-day EMA, supported by improving spot flows and reduced long-side leverage, would signal that the corrective phase is likely nearing completion.

Until that confirmation arrives, DOGE remains in a repair phase, quietly stabilizing rather than rebounding aggressively, as the market waits for a clear catalyst to define the next directional move.
